part_title: Section Four: Economic Thresholds and Intervention Decision-Making

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Monitoring does not aim merely to collect trap and larval counts. Its purpose is to convert these data into an appropriate management decision that prevents Tuta absoluta from reaching a level that causes economic loss.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring does not aim merely to collect trap and larval counts. Its purpose is to convert these data into an appropriate management decision that prevents Tuta absoluta from reaching a level that causes economic loss.

items.text: The presence of the pest in the field does not necessarily require insecticide use. The field may contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of the pest in the field does not necessarily require insecticide use. The field may contain:

items: A limited number of moths without successful oviposition.
Eggs with a high proportion exposed to parasitism or predation.
Low leaf infestation that the plant can compensate for.
A small focus that can be contained locally.
Natural enemies capable of slowing pest increase.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A limited number of moths without successful oviposition.
[[prepared]]Eggs with a high proportion exposed to [[term:biological-control]]parasitism[[/term]] or predation.
[[prepared]]Low leaf infestation that the plant can compensate for.
[[prepared]]A small focus that can be contained locally.
[[prepared]]Natural enemies capable of slowing pest increase.

items.text: Conversely, an apparently low pest density may require rapid intervention when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conversely, an apparently low pest density may require rapid intervention when:

items: The crop is at a sensitive stage.
The target market is highly sensitive to defects.
An immigration wave is arriving from a neighboring field.
Temperature rises and the hatching period becomes shorter.
Infestation occurs in growing points or clusters.
Harvest is approaching and insecticide options are restricted by preharvest intervals.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The crop is at a sensitive stage.
[[prepared]]The target market is highly sensitive to defects.
[[prepared]]An immigration wave is arriving from a neighboring field.
[[prepared]]Temperature rises and the hatching period becomes shorter.
[[prepared]]Infestation occurs in growing points or clusters.
[[prepared]]Harvest is approaching and insecticide options are restricted by preharvest intervals.

items.text: The decision must therefore be based on a group of indicators rather than on a single number.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision must therefore be based on a group of indicators rather than on a single number.

items.text: Basic Concepts
Economic Injury Level
Economic Injury Level — EIL
This is the lowest pest density at which the monetary value of the expected loss equals the cost of intervention.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the lowest pest density at which the monetary value of the expected loss equals the cost of intervention.

items.text: At this point:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At this point:

items.text: Value of the crop protected by intervention = Cost of implementing the intervention
If pest density continues to increase above the economic injury level, the expected loss becomes greater than the cost of control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If pest density continues to increase above the economic injury level, the expected loss becomes greater than the cost of control.

items.text: Intervention should not be delayed until the pest reaches this point because control requires time before its effect becomes apparent.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intervention should not be delayed until the pest reaches this point because control requires time before its effect becomes apparent.

items.text: Economic Threshold
Economic Threshold — ET
This is the pest density at which intervention should begin to prevent the population from reaching the economic injury level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the pest density at which intervention should begin to prevent the population from reaching the economic injury level.

items.text: The economic threshold is usually lower than the economic injury level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The [[term:economic-threshold]]economic threshold[[/term]] is usually lower than the economic injury level.

items.text: The relationship can be expressed simply as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The relationship can be expressed simply as:

items.text: Economic Threshold < Economic Injury Level
The economic threshold is also known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic threshold is also known as:

items.text: However, these terms may be used differently among studies, so the definition used in each study should be reviewed before comparing numerical values.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, these terms may be used differently among studies, so the definition used in each study should be reviewed before comparing numerical values.

items.text: Warning Level
This is a level below the intervention threshold indicating the need to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is a level below the intervention threshold indicating the need to:

items.text: The warning level does not mean immediate chemical treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The warning level does not mean immediate chemical treatment.

items.text: Decision Point
This is the moment at which all available information is reviewed to determine the most appropriate action.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the moment at which all available information is reviewed to determine the most appropriate action.

items.text: The decision may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision may be:

items.text: The intervention threshold is therefore not always equivalent to a “spray threshold.”
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The intervention threshold is therefore not always equivalent to a “spray threshold.”

items.text: General Equation for the Economic Injury Level
The economic injury level can be calculated using the general equation:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic injury level can be calculated using the general equation:

items.text: EIL = C ÷ (V × I × D × K)
Where:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Where:

rows: Cost of control per unit area
Market value per unit of crop
Amount of injury or damage caused by one pest individual
Crop loss resulting from one unit of injury
Proportion of pest reduction achieved by the intervention
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Amount of injury or damage caused by one pest individual
[[prepared]]Crop loss resulting from one unit of injury
[[prepared]]Proportion of pest reduction achieved by the intervention

items.text: The equation shows that the economic injury level is not a fixed number.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The equation shows that the economic injury level is not a fixed number.

items.text: When Crop Value Increases
The pest density justifying intervention decreases because loss of a small quantity of a high-value crop is costly.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest density justifying intervention decreases because loss of a small quantity of a high-value crop is costly.

items.text: When Control Cost Increases
The economic injury level increases because intervention becomes profitable only when a larger loss is expected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic injury level increases because intervention becomes profitable only when a larger loss is expected.

items.text: When the Control Method Is Highly Effective
The intervention threshold decreases relatively because the intervention can prevent a larger proportion of the loss.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The intervention threshold decreases relatively because the intervention can prevent a larger proportion of the loss.

items.text: When Control Efficacy Is Low
Intervention may not be economically justified even when infestation is present, or more than one method may need to be integrated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intervention may not be economically justified even when infestation is present, or more than one method may need to be integrated.

items.text: Why Is There No Universal Fixed Threshold for Tuta absoluta?
The economic threshold varies among countries, regions, and fields because of differences in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic threshold varies among countries, regions, and fields because of differences in:

items.text: One study may express infestation as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study may express infestation as:

items.text: Another may use:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Another may use:

items.text: These units cannot be converted directly into one another without a locally established experimental relationship.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These units cannot be converted directly into one another without a locally established experimental relationship.

items.text: Difference Between Leaf-Based and Fruit-Based Thresholds
Leaf injury and fruit infestation must be considered separately.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaf injury and fruit infestation must be considered separately.

items.text: Leaf-Based Threshold
This measures:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This measures:

items.text: Tomato plants may compensate for part of the leaf injury, particularly during early vegetative growth.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tomato plants may compensate for part of the leaf injury, particularly during early vegetative growth.

items.text: Fruit-Based Threshold
This is more sensitive because an infested fruit may become unmarketable or unsuitable for processing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is more sensitive because an infested fruit may become unmarketable or unsuitable for processing.

items.text: A small percentage of fruit infestation may be more costly than a higher percentage of leaf mining.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A small percentage of fruit infestation may be more costly than a higher percentage of leaf mining.

items.text: The intervention threshold therefore declines as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The intervention threshold therefore declines as:

items: Production is intended for export or premium markets.
The probability of secondary rots increases.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Production is intended for export or premium markets.

items.text: Thresholds Published in Studies
Greenhouse Study in Ethiopia
One greenhouse study estimated:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One greenhouse study estimated:

items: Economic injury level: approximately 3 larvae per plant.
Economic threshold: approximately 2.25 larvae per plant.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Economic injury level: approximately 3 larvae per plant.

items.text: The economic threshold was calculated to allow intervention before the population reached the economic injury level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic threshold was calculated to allow intervention before the population reached the economic injury level.

items.text: This value should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open fields because it was developed under:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This value should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open fields because it was developed under:

items.text: Open-Field Study in Iran
A study estimating crop losses on different cultivars found that the economic injury level ranged approximately between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A study estimating crop losses on different cultivars found that the economic injury level ranged approximately between:

items.text: 4.15 and 5.04 larvae per plant
The value differed among cultivars, confirming that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The value differed among cultivars, confirming that:

items: The threshold is not a fixed property of the pest alone.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The threshold is not a fixed property of the pest alone.

items.text: Trap-Based Thresholds
Some older systems used advisory male-catch values such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some older systems used advisory male-catch values such as:

items: 1–3 moths per trap per week: low risk.
4–30 moths per trap per week: moderate risk.
More than 30 moths per trap per week: high risk.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1–3 moths per trap per week: low risk.
[[prepared]]4–30 moths per trap per week: moderate risk.
[[prepared]]More than 30 moths per trap per week: high risk.

items.text: Some studies or extension programs reported much higher values, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some studies or extension programs reported much higher values, such as:

items: Approximately 45 moths or more per trap per day in one Brazilian program.
Approximately 100 males per trap per day in some older recommendations.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Approximately 45 moths or more per trap per day in one Brazilian program.
[[prepared]]Approximately 100 males per trap per day in some older recommendations.

items.text: These differences do not necessarily mean that one value is correct and another is wrong. They reflect differences in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These differences do not necessarily mean that one value is correct and another is wrong. They reflect differences in:

items: Local relationship between male catch and damage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Local relationship between male catch and damage.

items.text: Trap counts should therefore not be used alone as a fixed economic threshold.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap counts should therefore not be used alone as a fixed economic threshold.

items.text: Limitations of Using Traps for Decision-Making
Pheromone traps primarily capture males, whereas actual damage depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]][[term:pheromone-trap]]Pheromone traps[[/term]] primarily capture males, whereas actual damage depends on:

items: Ability of larvae to reach fruits.

items.text: The following situations may occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following situations may occur:

items.text: High Catch with Low Plant Infestation
Because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Because of:

items: Immigration of males from outside the field.
High levels of parasitism and predation.
Failure of part of the eggs to hatch.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Immigration of males from outside the field.
[[prepared]]Failure of part of the eggs to hatch.

items.text: Low Catch with High Plant Infestation
Because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Because of:

items: Larvae resulting from a previous wave.
Trap saturation or loss of adhesiveness.

items.text: Traps are therefore used to determine:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps are therefore used to determine:

items.text: They should not be used alone to issue a spray decision.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should not be used alone to issue a spray decision.

items.text: Indicators That Must Be Integrated into the Decision
Management decisions are based on six groups of indicators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management decisions are based on six groups of indicators.

items.text: First: Adult-Insect Indicators

items: Proximity of an old or neglected field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Proximity of an old or neglected field.

items.text: Second: Egg Indicators

items: Location of eggs on the plant.

items.text: Third: Larval Indicators

items: Number of live larvae per plant.
Presence of larvae in growing points or stems.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of larvae in growing points or stems.

items.text: Fourth: Fruit Indicators
Fifth: Crop Indicators
Sixth: Natural-Regulation Indicators

items: Presence of larvae killed by pathogens.
Effect of previous insecticides on natural enemies.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Effect of previous insecticides on natural enemies.

items.text: Decision-Making According to Crop Stage
Transplant and Early-Growth Stage
Priority is given to protecting:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Priority is given to protecting:

items.text: Limited growing-point infestation may require faster intervention than a similar number of mines on leaves of a large plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limited growing-point infestation may require faster intervention than a similar number of mines on leaves of a large plant.

items.text: Risk increases when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Risk increases when:

items: Infestation is distributed throughout the field.
The main growing point is infested.
Infestation was confirmed to have arrived with transplants.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Infestation was confirmed to have arrived with transplants.

items.text: Vegetative-Growth Stage
The plant has relatively greater capacity to compensate for limited leaf injury.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant has relatively greater capacity to compensate for limited leaf injury.

items.text: The following should be considered:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be considered:

items: Rate of appearance of new mines.

items.text: The presence of old dry mines alone does not justify treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of old dry mines alone does not justify treatment.

items.text: Flowering and Fruit-Set Stage
Tolerance of infestation declines because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tolerance of infestation declines because:

items: Advanced larvae move toward the calyx and fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Advanced larvae move toward the calyx and fruits.

items.text: Greater weight should be given in the decision to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Greater weight should be given in the decision to:

items.text: Fruit-Filling Stage
Priority shifts to preventing fruit infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Priority shifts to preventing fruit infestation.

items.text: A low number of advanced larvae near clusters may justify faster intervention than a higher number of young larvae in leaves distant from fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A low number of advanced larvae near clusters may justify faster intervention than a higher number of young larvae in leaves distant from fruits.

items.text: The following should be considered:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be considered:

items.text: Near Harvest
The economic value of intervention changes as the remaining period becomes shorter.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic value of intervention changes as the remaining period becomes shorter.

items.text: Consider:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Consider:

items: Speed of action of the method.
Presence of a new hatching wave.
Value of the crop that can still be protected.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Value of the crop that can still be protected.

items.text: Biological intervention or removal of foci and infested fruits may be more appropriate than an insecticide with a long preharvest interval.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological intervention or removal of foci and infested fruits may be more appropriate than an insecticide with a long preharvest interval.

items.text: Difference Between Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes
Fresh-Market Tomatoes
The threshold for fruit infestation is very low, especially when production is intended for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The threshold for fruit infestation is very low, especially when production is intended for:

items.text: A single hole may downgrade or reject a fruit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A single hole may downgrade or reject a fruit.

items.text: The decision should therefore be taken before widespread fruit infestation appears.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should therefore be taken before widespread fruit infestation appears.

items.text: Processing Tomatoes
Processing tomatoes can tolerate a limited level of leaf injury more than fresh-market tomatoes, provided that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Processing tomatoes can tolerate a limited level of leaf injury more than fresh-market tomatoes, provided that:

items.text: Fruit infestation remains highly important because it causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruit infestation remains highly important because it causes:

items.text: In processing tomatoes, the intervention decision should focus on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In processing tomatoes, the intervention decision should focus on:

items.text: Practical Decision Matrix

rows: Search for immigration source; do not spray automatically
Give priority to biological and selective methods
Reduce pest pressure and prevent expansion
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Search for immigration source; do not spray automatically
[[prepared]]Give priority to biological and selective methods

items.text: Practical Decision Levels
Level One: Continue Monitoring
Applied when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Applied when:

items: No confirmed plant infestation is present.
No dangerous immigration sources are present.

items.text: Action:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Action:

Applied when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Applied when:

items.text: Action:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Action:

items.text: Level Three: Preventive or Localized Intervention
Applied when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Applied when:

items: Active mines occur in a small sector.
Larvae are still in early instars.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Active mines occur in a small sector.

items.text: Action:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Action:

items: Use a biological or selective method.
Avoid treating the entire field unnecessarily.

items.text: Level Four: Integrated Intervention
Applied when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Applied when:

items.text: Action:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Action:

items.text: Level Five: Rescue Intervention
Applied when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Applied when:

items.text: Action:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Action:

items: Remove severely infested fruits and foci.
Review active ingredient, coverage, and resistance.
Prevent the field from becoming an infestation source.
Assess the economic feasibility of continuing.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent the field from becoming an infestation source.

items.text: Effect of Natural Enemies on the Threshold
The same larval density should not be treated identically when natural-enemy activity differs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The same larval density should not be treated identically when natural-enemy activity differs.

items.text: The presence of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of:

items: A high percentage of parasitized eggs.
Larvae killed by fungi or bacteria.
A continuing decline in new mines.

items.text: May permit chemical intervention to be delayed while monitoring is intensified.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]May permit chemical intervention to be delayed while monitoring is intensified.

items.text: Conversely, absence of natural enemies or their exposure to a broad-spectrum insecticide increases the probability of rapid pest increase.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conversely, absence of natural enemies or their exposure to a broad-spectrum insecticide increases the probability of rapid pest increase.

items.text: A decision to delay intervention should not be based merely on observing one predator, but on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A decision to delay intervention should not be based merely on observing one predator, but on:

items.text: Effect of Temperature on Decision Timing
As temperature rises within the pest’s suitable range:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As temperature rises within the pest’s suitable range:

items.text: Under moderately warm temperatures, eggs may hatch within only a few days. Delaying inspection for a full week after detecting a large number of eggs may therefore be insufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under moderately warm temperatures, eggs may hatch within only a few days. Delaying inspection for a full week after detecting a large number of eggs may therefore be insufficient.

items.text: Under cool conditions, development is slower, but infestation does not disappear, and larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under cool conditions, development is slower, but infestation does not disappear, and larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods.

items.text: Inspection frequency should be adjusted according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection frequency should be adjusted according to:

items.text: Effect of Neighboring Fields
The field may remain below the internal intervention threshold while a severely infested adjacent old field raises the expected risk.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The field may remain below the internal intervention threshold while a severely infested adjacent old field raises the expected risk.

items.text: The decision should then also include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should then also include:

items: Proximity of removal of the old field.
Cessation of control in that field.
Presence of recent eggs in the new field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Proximity of removal of the old field.
[[prepared]]Presence of recent eggs in the new field.

items.text: The most appropriate action may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most appropriate action may be:

items.text: The new field should not be treated in isolation while the immigration source remains unmanaged.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The new field should not be treated in isolation while the immigration source remains unmanaged.

items.text: Localized Treatment or Whole-Field Treatment?
Localized treatment is used when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Localized treatment is used when:

items: Infestation is limited to a clearly defined area.
No general immigration wave is present.
The focus can be reinspected rapidly.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Infestation is limited to a clearly defined area.

items.text: It may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may include:

items: Removal of infested leaves or growing points.
Increasing trap density near the source.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Removal of infested leaves or growing points.

items.text: Whole-field treatment should be considered when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Whole-field treatment should be considered when:

items: Trap catches rise at several locations.
Plants are close together and the focus cannot be isolated.
A general hatching wave is occurring.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Plants are close together and the focus cannot be isolated.

items.text: Uncontrolled reliance on localized treatment will not protect the rest of the field when infestation is already widespread.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Uncontrolled reliance on localized treatment will not protect the rest of the field when infestation is already widespread.

items.text: Timing of Intervention in Relation to Larval Instar
Eggs
They may be targeted through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may be targeted through:

items.text: First and Second Instars
These are the best larval stages to target because they are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are the best larval stages to target because they are:

items: More susceptible to several biological and selective products.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]More susceptible to several biological and selective products.

items.text: Third and Fourth Instars
Control becomes more difficult because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control becomes more difficult because of:

items: Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits.

items.text: Management should therefore not be based on waiting for large larvae to appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management should therefore not be based on waiting for large larvae to appear.

items.text: Evaluation of the Decision After Intervention
The field should be reinspected after a period appropriate to the method used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The field should be reinspected after a period appropriate to the method used.

items.text: Success is measured through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Success is measured through:

items: Stabilization or reduction of fruit infestation.

items.text: Success is not measured through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Success is not measured through:

items: A decrease in trap catch for only one or two days.
Observation of dead larvae without assessment of new infestation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A decrease in trap catch for only one or two days.
[[prepared]]Observation of dead larvae without assessment of new infestation.

items.text: Reasons for Intervention Failure Despite a Correct Decision
The decision to intervene may be correct, but implementation may fail because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision to intervene may be correct, but implementation may fail because of:

items: Selection of a product unsuitable for the stage present.
Delayed application until larvae entered fruits.
Repetition of a resistant mode-of-action group.
High densities of pupae and eggs unaffected by the treatment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Selection of a product unsuitable for the stage present.
[[prepared]]High densities of [[term:pupation]]pupae[[/term]] and eggs unaffected by the treatment.

items.text: The following must therefore be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following must therefore be distinguished:

items.text: Practical Decision Framework for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
No single validated threshold can be applied to every tomato season in Egypt. A decision framework combining several indicators should therefore be used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single validated threshold can be applied to every tomato season in Egypt. A decision framework combining several indicators should therefore be used.

items.text: Situation One: No Live Larvae
Even when a limited number of moths occurs in traps:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Even when a limited number of moths occurs in traps:

items.text: Situation Two: Limited Eggs Without Mines

items: Use appropriate preventive or biological methods.

items.text: Situation Three: Limited Active Mines

items: Remove heavily infested leaves when appropriate.

items.text: Situation Four: Distributed Live Larvae
Situation Five: Larvae Near Fruits

items: Inspect the area beneath the calyx.

items.text: Situation Six: Beginning of Fruit Infestation
Situation Seven: Harvest Approaching

items: Calculate the value of crop that can still be protected.
Use methods compatible with delivery requirements.
Avoid spraying that provides no economic return or creates a residue problem.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Calculate the value of crop that can still be protected.
[[prepared]]Avoid spraying that provides no economic return or creates a residue problem.

items.text: Weekly Decision Algorithm
Step One: Review Traps

items: Which trap has the highest catch?

items.text: Step Two: Inspect Plants

items: How many live larvae are present?

items.text: Step Three: Inspect Sensitive Plant Organs
Step Four: Assess Natural Regulation

items: What percentage of eggs is parasitized?

items.text: Step Five: Assess the Crop

items: How many days remain until harvest?
What is the value of the crop at risk?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]What is the value of the crop at risk?

items.text: Step Six: Select the Action
Step Seven: Set the Reassessment Date
The reassessment date should be set before leaving the field, and the decision should not be left without follow-up.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The reassessment date should be set before leaving the field, and the decision should not be left without follow-up.

items.text: Decision Card Template
Common Errors in Threshold Use
Error One: Using a Number from Another Country Without Adjustment
Prices, cultivars, and production conditions differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Prices, cultivars, and production conditions differ.

items.text: Error Two: Using Traps Alone
Trap catches may not reflect larval density or fruit infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap catches may not reflect larval density or fruit infestation.

items.text: Error Three: Treating a Risk Level as a Spray Threshold
Classification as low, moderate, or high risk does not automatically equal a chemical-treatment decision.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Classification as low, moderate, or high risk does not automatically equal a chemical-treatment decision.

items.text: Error Four: Ignoring Crop Stage
The same infestation is more serious during fruit set and fruiting.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The same infestation is more serious during fruit set and fruiting.

items.text: Error Five: Counting Old Mines
Decisions should be based on active mines and live larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Decisions should be based on active mines and live larvae.

items.text: Error Six: Ignoring Natural Enemies
This may lead to unnecessary intervention.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may lead to unnecessary intervention.

items.text: Error Seven: Waiting for Fruit Infestation
Intervention should occur before larvae reach fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intervention should occur before larvae reach fruits.

items.text: Error Eight: Using the Field Average and Ignoring Foci
A focus may spread before its effect appears in the overall average.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A focus may spread before its effect appears in the overall average.

items.text: Error Nine: Treating Every Intervention as Spraying
The most appropriate action may be cultural, biological, or localized.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most appropriate action may be cultural, biological, or localized.

items.text: Error Ten: Failing to Reassess
Decision success cannot be determined without follow-up inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Decision success cannot be determined without follow-up inspection.

items.text: Error Eleven: Reducing the Dose to Lower Cost
An ineffective dose may increase both loss and resistance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An ineffective dose may increase both loss and resistance.

items.text: Error Twelve: Ignoring the Preharvest Interval
An entomologically correct intervention may cause delivery and residue problems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An entomologically correct intervention may cause delivery and residue problems.

items.text: Indicators of a Successful Decision System
The system is successful when it results in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The system is successful when it results in:

items: Prevention or reduction of fruit infestation.
A record explaining the reason for each intervention.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A record explaining the reason for each intervention.

items.text: Summary of Section Four

The economic injury level is the pest density at which the value of crop loss equals the cost of control, whereas the economic threshold is the point at which action begins before the population reaches the injury level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic injury level is the pest density at which the value of crop loss equals the cost of control, whereas the economic threshold is the point at which action begins before the population reaches the injury level.

items.text: There is no single universal threshold for Tuta absoluta because thresholds are affected by cultivar, environment, market, price, control cost, crop stage, and measurement unit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no single universal threshold for Tuta absoluta because thresholds are affected by cultivar, environment, market, price, control cost, crop stage, and measurement unit.

items.text: Published studies have recorded different thresholds, including 2.25 larvae per plant in one greenhouse study and economic injury levels ranging from approximately 4.15 to 5.04 larvae per plant on different cultivars in open fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Published studies have recorded different thresholds, including 2.25 larvae per plant in one greenhouse study and economic injury levels ranging from approximately 4.15 to 5.04 larvae per plant on different cultivars in open fields.

items.text: These values should not be transferred directly to all Egyptian conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values should not be transferred directly to all Egyptian conditions.

items.text: Management decisions in open fields should integrate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management decisions in open fields should integrate:

items.text: The intervention threshold does not necessarily mean insecticide spraying. The decision may be to remove a focus, release a natural enemy, use a microbial product, or manage an immigration source.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The intervention threshold does not necessarily mean insecticide spraying. The decision may be to remove a focus, release a natural enemy, use a microbial product, or manage an immigration source.

items.text: Tolerance of infestation declines at fruit set and fruiting, and the threshold for fruit infestation is much lower than the threshold for leaf injury.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tolerance of infestation declines at fruit set and fruiting, and the threshold for fruit infestation is much lower than the threshold for leaf injury.

items.text: Every decision should be followed by reassessment based on new mines, live larvae, and fruit infestation rather than on the persistence of old visible damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every decision should be followed by reassessment based on new mines, live larvae, and fruit infestation rather than on the persistence of old visible damage.
